body.title: bin/superclaude: sync tmux session name with hostname.cc.0 naming
body.result: bin/superclaude: hostname-based N-detection replaces hardcoded 'claude-main'; exports CC_TMUX_SESSION explicitly into both env and CLAUDE_CMD string
body.description:
**Problem (retroactive from T-cc-session-hostname-naming):** bin/superclaude hardcodes `CLAUDE_TMUX_SESSION=${CLAUDE_TMUX_SESSION:-claude-main}` and creates the tmux session as 'claude-main'. But constants.py `_detect_session_name()` returns `<hostname>.cc.0` (since 'claude-main' doesn't match the `*.cc.N` pattern). Result: `TMUX_SESSION = akiendelllinux.cc.0` but the actual session is 'claude-main' — any CCWorkerListener nag or HALT send-keys call targets the wrong name and silently fails.

**What to fix:** In bin/superclaude, replace the hardcoded 'claude-main' default with the hostname-based name:
```bash
_HOSTNAME=$(hostname | cut -d. -f1 | tr '[:upper:]' '[:lower:]')
CLAUDE_TMUX_SESSION="${CC_TMUX_SESSION:-${_HOSTNAME}.cc.0}"
export CC_TMUX_SESSION="$CLAUDE_TMUX_SESSION"
```
This makes CC_TMUX_SESSION available in the launched Claude environment so constants.py env-var path fires instead of _detect_session_name().

**Affected files:** bin/superclaude (bash)
**Design rules:** External state — session name is an external state parameter; CC should never auto-detect when it can be told explicitly
**Scope boundary:** bin/superclaude only. No changes to constants.py (it already handles CC_TMUX_SESSION correctly).
**Completion criteria:** After the fix, `tmux list-sessions` shows `akiendelllinux.cc.0` (not 'claude-main'). CC_TMUX_SESSION env var is set inside the CC session. CCWorkerListener nag targets the correct session.
